local_names.romanized_local: Hēi Qiǎo Kè Lì (Chinese); Ṭārk Cāklēṭ (Tamil)

aliases: 黑巧克力 (Hēi Qiǎo Kè Lì)

chinese: 黑巧克力 (Hēi Qiǎo Kè Lì)

region_of_origin: Mesoamerican origin (cacao — Theobroma cacao); modern 70%+ dark chocolate formulation is European (Swiss, Belgian, French)
food_subgroup: Dark chocolate with ≥70% cocoa content; minimum 70% cocoa required for clinically meaningful flavanol content

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has entries for chocolate products but not specifically for 70% cocoa dark chocolate. Closest parent is confectionery product (chocolate food product). No exact URI available without fabrication.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for 70% cocoa dark chocolate without risk of fabrication.

allergens: may contain milk traces (depending on brand)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard clinical serving of dark chocolate is 2 breakable squares (approximately 20g) from a 100g bar, providing approximately 120 kcal, 150–200mg cocoa flavanols, and a glycemic load of only ~2.

nutrient_source.title: Dark chocolate, 70–85% cacao solids — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170272)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170272 is specifically for dark chocolate 70–85% cacao solids, which directly matches the ≥70% cocoa specification of this record. All per_100g values are sourced from this entry.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Dark Chocolate (70% cocoa) — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~20–23 (average ~22) for 70% dark chocolate per University of Sydney GI Database. GL calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (22 × 8.76g per 20g serving) / 100 ≈ 1.9. Classified as low GI (≤55) and low GL (<10).

hypertension.key_factor: Cocoa flavanols stimulate endothelial nitric oxide synthase (eNOS), increasing nitric oxide-mediated vasodilation; magnesium (50mg per 20g serving = ~13% RDI) supports vascular relaxation; sodium is very low at 4mg per 20g serving.
hypertension.guidance: A daily serving of 2 squares (20g) of ≥70% dark chocolate is appropriate for hypertensive patients; multiple RCTs demonstrate a reduction in systolic blood pressure of approximately 2–3 mmHg via flavanol-mediated nitric oxide production and improved endothelial function (brachial artery flow-mediated dilation +1.3–2%).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~22) and glycemic load (~2 per 20g serving); cocoa flavanols improve insulin sensitivity via AMPK activation and reduce postprandial glucose via inhibition of alpha-glucosidase and alpha-amylase.
type2_diabetes.guidance: A 20g serving (2 squares) of ≥70% dark chocolate is permissible for patients with Type 2 Diabetes given its very low GI (~22) and GL (~2); cocoa flavanols have demonstrated improvements in insulin sensitivity in RCTs (e.g., Grassi et al., AJCN 2005), though the strongest evidence is at higher doses (100g/day) and portion control remains essential.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cocoa flavanols (epicatechin, catechin) reduce LDL oxidation, inhibit platelet aggregation, and improve endothelial function; the predominant saturated fat is stearic acid, which is metabolically converted to oleic acid in the liver and is cardiovascular-neutral.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A daily serving of 20g of ≥70% dark chocolate is cardioprotective at the population level; flavanols reduce LDL oxidation and platelet aggregation, and the high stearic acid content does not raise LDL cholesterol, making it a clinically acceptable indulgence for cardiovascular disease patients when consumed in the 20g serving size.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Cocoa flavanols inhibit NF-κB signalling and TNF-alpha production; magnesium supports anti-inflammatory enzyme function; iron (1.3mg per 20g) is relevant for RA-associated anaemia.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dark chocolate (≥70% cocoa) may offer mild anti-inflammatory benefit for rheumatoid arthritis patients via flavanol-mediated NF-κB inhibition, though clinical evidence is currently limited to in-vitro and small observational studies rather than RCTs.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dark chocolate with ≥70% cocoa content is a processed confectionery product made from cocoa mass, cocoa butter, sugar, and vanilla, produced through fermentation, roasting, conching, and tempering of Theobroma cacao beans. It is dark brown in colour, firm in texture, and characterised by a complex bitter-sweet flavour profile with earthy, fruity, and slightly astringent notes. The 70% minimum cocoa threshold is clinically critical: it ensures meaningful concentrations of cocoa flavanols (epicatechin and catechin), which are largely absent in milk chocolate (~35% cocoa). A standard clinical serving is 2 squares (20g) from a 100g bar, providing approximately 120 kcal, 150–200mg cocoa flavanols, 50mg magnesium (~13% RDI), 1.3mg iron (~7% RDI), and a glycemic load of only ~2. The GI is very low (~22). Cardiovascular benefits documented in RCTs include systolic blood pressure reduction (~2–3 mmHg), improved endothelial function via nitric oxide, LDL oxidation reduction, and anti-platelet aggregation. The predominant saturated fat is stearic acid, which is metabolically converted to oleic acid in the liver and does not raise LDL cholesterol — making it unique among saturated fats. In Singapore, Lindt 70% Excellence is the most widely available brand meeting the ≥70% threshold, sold at NTUC FairPrice, Cold Storage, and Market Place. Dark chocolate occupies a unique position in clinical nutrition as perhaps the only indulgence food that can be recommended with confidence to patients with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease at the 20g serving size.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170272 (dark chocolate 70–85% cacao solids), which is an exact match for the ≥70% cocoa specification. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the approved primary source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown (MUFA/PUFA split) not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170272 for this entry; water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the primary source entry consulted. trans_fat_g is confirmed 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170272. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are confirmed 0.0 per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170272 — these are naturally zero or below detection threshold in high-cocoa dark chocolate. No zero-macro justification required as protein_g (7.8), fat_g (42.6), and carbohydrate_g (45.8) are all non-zero. GI of ~22 is from University of Sydney GI Database for 70% dark chocolate; GL per 20g serving calculated as (22 × 8.76) / 100 ≈ 1.9. The saturated fat in dark chocolate is predominantly stearic acid, which does not raise LDL cholesterol. FoodEx2 code A0CKN (Dark chocolate) used as the primary ontology anchor. FoodOn URI not assigned to avoid fabrication; closest parent is chocolate food product. SNOMED CT code not assigned to avoid fabrication.
